concept: Wash / Cleanse with Water / Restore to Clean
description: To wash: hands before bread, cups after tea, cloth in the river. Water restoring things to cleanness. Its family is 'phialu' (water), 'hiso' (clean), and 'therilu' (rest) — washing is how objects rest and return.
sound_symbolism: The water-hiss 's' runs through the doubled openness of 'awa' — the word pours. Say it and hear the basin.
grammatical_notes: Transitive: 'mia lo soeli sawa.' (I wash the cups.) 'lo mia wetha mua luphore sawa.' (We wash cloth in the stream.) Reflexively with 'miso': 'mia miso sawa.' (I wash myself.)

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Washing is the most repeated ritual of ordinary life — attention restoring the ordinary to freshness, one bowl at a time. Zen kitchens made it doctrine; households made it first.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: The river's laundry, the well's bucket, the rain barrel: water-work bound the day to the water source and the neighbors met there.

semantic_domains.ritual: The small ceremony of care for things
